rationale: An approval gate enforced at the tool-execution boundary is the architectural form of 'effectively overseen by natural persons during use' for an action-taking agent — it satisfies the oversight design duty in part; organizational measures complete it.
An immutable per-action log does not itself provide oversight, but it is the evidence layer that makes oversight demonstrable — who approved what, when, within which policy.

The disclosure half of the labeling surface (in-product 'you are interacting with an AI system' copy) implements Art 50(1)'s design duty for direct-interaction systems; the obviousness qualifier is a legal judgment left to counsel.

A meaningful human decision point on consequential outcomes is the classic route out of 'decision based solely on automated processing' — the gate implements the general duty; whether review is 'meaningful' remains a facts-and-circumstances question.
Logging the human's role per decision (approver, basis, timing) is the evidence that a decision was not solely automated.
The conspicuous-mark half of the labeling surface implements Art 17's 显著标识 duty for enumerated functions including 智能对话 — rendering-layer marks on the agent's dialogue surface.

The dual-layer design (explicit render-layer marks + implicit file-metadata marks) maps one-to-one onto the 显式/隐式 definitions — a single choke point configured to the strictest regime serves CN 2025, CN Art 17, and EU Art 50(1) together.
Content-producer responsibility is dischargeable only with per-output evidence — the action log ties each generated artifact to its moderation and labeling steps.
The takedown/rollback path in the incident runbook operationalizes the provider's information-security obligations when generated content must be pulled.

The capability-profile document with data-flow map is the artifact a developer hands a deployer — it is the technical documentation the ADMT duty asks for, maintained continuously rather than assembled at deadline.
